title: Feature Extractor vs. Full Fine-Tuning
concept: In transfer learning, a pretrained network can be used as a fixed feature extractor or updated end to end with full fine-tuning, and the better choice depends on data size, domain shift, and compute budget.

exercises.prompt: Recap: Why might you fine-tune later layers instead of only freezing them?

options.text: Because higher-level features may need to adapt to the new task
Because early layers can never be useful again
Because pretrained models cannot make predictions until fully erased

exercises.explain: Exactly. This lesson compares that adaptation-heavy approach with using the pretrained model as a fixed feature extractor.

cards.title: Borrow the expert or retrain the expert
cards.body: Sometimes you hire an expert and let them work with almost no changes. Other times you retrain them deeply for a new role. Transfer learning has those two moods too: use a pretrained network mainly as-is, or fine-tune much more of it.

cards.title: Two common transfer-learning modes
cards.body: In feature extractor mode, most or all pretrained layers stay fixed and provide learned features to a new classifier head. In full fine-tuning, you continue training many or all pretrained weights so the whole representation can shift toward the new task. The choice depends on how much new data you have, how different the new domain is, and how much compute you can spend.

cards.title: Furnished rental vs. full renovation
cards.body: Moving into a furnished apartment is fast: keep most things, make a few local changes, and start living there. A full renovation takes more work but can fit unusual needs better. Feature extraction is the furnished move; full fine-tuning is the renovation.

cards.title: Factory defects with different data situations
cards.body: If a factory only has a few hundred labeled defect images, using a pretrained model as a feature extractor is often a safe first baseline. If the factory later gathers a much larger dataset and its images look very different from the original pretraining data, full fine-tuning becomes more attractive because the whole network can adapt.

cards.title: Full fine-tuning is not automatically best
cards.body: A common myth is that updating more weights must always produce a better model. But with very limited data, full fine-tuning can over-adjust the pretrained model or waste compute. Sometimes the simpler feature-extractor setup is the better practical choice.

cards.title: Light reuse vs. deep adaptation
cards.body: Both are transfer learning, but they borrow pretrained knowledge in different ways.

left.text:
Most pretrained layers frozen
Train only a small new head
Often good for small datasets and quick baselines

right.text:
Many or all pretrained layers update
Representation can shift more deeply
Often useful with more data or bigger domain change

exercises.prompt: Which transfer-learning mode usually changes fewer weights?
exercises.explain: Correct. Feature extractor mode usually freezes most pretrained layers and changes only a small top section.

exercises.prompt: True or false: With a tiny dataset, using a pretrained model as a fixed feature extractor is often a sensible starting point.
exercises.explain: True. When data is limited, the simpler frozen-backbone setup is often a strong practical baseline.

exercises.sentence: When you update many or all pretrained weights on the new task, you are doing full ___.
exercises.explain: Exactly. Full fine-tuning means the pretrained representation itself is still being reshaped.

exercises.prompt: Which situation best matches feature extractor mode?

options.text: Keeping the pretrained backbone fixed and training only a new classification head for a small photo dataset
Updating every layer heavily even though you only have a handful of examples
Ignoring the pretrained model and starting from random weights instead

exercises.explain: Right. Feature extractor mode means mostly reusing the backbone as-is and learning a smaller top layer.

exercises.prompt: A team has 1,000 labeled images, limited GPU time, and wants a fast first transfer-learning baseline. Which mode is the better first choice?

options.text: Use the pretrained network as a feature extractor and train a new head first
Always full-fine-tune every weight immediately because more updates are always better
Throw away the pretrained model and begin from random weights
Skip training and rely on the original labels from pretraining

exercises.explain: Exactly. With small data and limited compute, feature extractor mode is often the safest first move.
